chunk_summary: This section explores pediatric CKD nutrition pitfalls through case vignettes, focusing on sodium depletion, hyperkalemia, and hyperphosphatemia management strategies in children with CKD or on dialysis.
learning_objectives: Identify and manage sodium depletion in infants with CKD and poor growth trajectories.
Understand the risks of potassium chloride additives in low-sodium diets for adolescents with CKD.
Optimize phosphate binder timing and dietary strategies for children on peritoneal dialysis with hyperphosphatemia.
key_facts: Sodium-wasting CKD can suppress appetite and growth despite normal serum sodium levels.
Potassium chloride additives in marketed low-sodium salts can cause hyperkalemia (potassium levels 5.8–6.2 mmol/L).
Phosphate binders must be taken with phosphate-containing meals/snacks to effectively manage hyperphosphatemia.
Measured sodium supplementation can improve hydration and growth in infants with CKD.
Persistent hyperphosphatemia in children on PD may require removal of phosphate additives and reassessment of calcium load.
reference_quote: The Pediatric Renal Nutrition Taskforce recommends measured sodium supplementation for infants with sodium-wasting CKD to improve growth and hydration. (Source: https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00467-025-06913-z)
